actions.thought: In Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em, playing from the Button (Small Blind) requires a wide range. 87o is a speculative off-suit connector. With 100BB effective stacks, raising can isolate the opponent but risks facing a 3-bet which forces a fold. Limping (calling) is a strong option here because I get 3:1 pot odds to see the flop and retain the advantage of position post-flop without inflating the pot with a marginal hand. It is slightly too strong to fold but slightly too weak to raise for value on the first hand of the match.

Kd 9d is a strong suited King in a heads-up context. Since the opponent only completed the small blind instead of raising, I want to take the initiative and put pressure on their capped range. Raising to 300 is a standard size to build the pot and potentially take it down immediately.

I am holding 87o on the Button (SB) and previously limped. The opponent in the Big Blind has raised to 300 (3BB). I am facing a raise of 200 more into a pot of 400, giving me 3:1 pot odds (25% equity required). Although I have the advantage of position post-flop and the odds are mathematically tempting, 87o is an off-suit connector that often suffers from reverse implied odds and poor playability (low equity realization) against a strong isolation range. In a standard Heads-Up limp strategy, 87o falls into the limp-fold category against a 3x or larger raise, as opposed to suited connectors or stronger hands which would defend. Since this is the first hand and I have no reads on the opponent's aggression frequency, the disciplined play is to fold.

Holding Queen-Ten of hearts on the button in a heads-up situation is a very strong hand. I will put in a standard raise to 300 chips to build the pot and take control of the hand with my positional advantage.

I am in the Big Blind with Ts5s facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button. The pot odds are calling 200 to win a total pot of 600, requiring 33% equity. While T5s has roughly 36% raw equity against a wide button opening range, realizing that equity Out of Position (OOP) against a larger sizing is difficult. With an equity realization factor likely below 1.0, my realized equity drops below the required threshold. Against a 3x open, T5s is generally just below the calling threshold compared to a min-raise. Therefore, folding is the disciplined play.

I am holding pocket Fours [4d, 4h] on the Button (Small Blind). In heads-up play, any pocket pair is a respectable starting hand. My opponent has shown aggression in the first two hands, raising pre-flop in both instances. Limping would likely invite another raise, putting me in a defensive spot. By raising to 200, I take the initiative, utilize my position, and force the opponent to react. This is a standard opening with a low pair 100BB deep.

I am in the Big Blind with KQo, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. The opponent raised to 2BB from the button. While I could just call and play a pot out of position with good equity, I prefer to 3-bet here for value and to take initiative. This also tests the opponent's opening range. A raise to 600 (3x the open) is a standard sizing to put pressure on his wider button steals and potentially take down the pot immediately.

I hold pocket Fours [4d, 4h] and face a 3-bet to 600 from the Big Blind. In heads-up play, pocket pairs have decent value. The opponent has been aggressive in all three hands so far, suggesting a wide range. The price to call is 400 into a pot that will be 1200, giving me 3:1 odds. Against a wide 3-betting range, 44 has sufficient equity to call. Additionally, being in position 100BB deep allows me to navigate post-flop effectively. 4-betting is unnecessary as it isolates me against a stronger range, and folding is too tight given the odds and hand strength. Calling is the standard optimal play.

I am the pre-flop 3-better and currently out of position on a Th-5s-5d board. This board is relatively dry, although it may have connected with some of the opponent's 3-bet calling range like pocket pairs or suited connectors. However, as the aggressor, I have a range advantage including overpairs (JJ+) and strong Tens (AT, KTs). I will lead with a standard continuation bet of 1/3 pot (400) to maintain initiative, fold out marginal hands, and define the opponent's range while keeping the pot manageable with my two overcards.

I hold pocket Fours [4d, 4h] on a [Th, 5s, 5d] board. After calling a pre-flop 3-bet, I am facing a continuation bet of 400 into a pot of 1200. While I am in position, the board texture heavily favors the opponent's 3-betting range, which includes overpairs (JJ+) and strong Tens (AT+). My hand is extremely vulnerable; I am currently ahead of unpaired high cards (like AK/AQ), but I lose to any pair 66+ and any Ten. Furthermore, I have only 2 outs to improve and will face difficult decisions on future streets if overcards appear. Since 44 represents the very bottom of my range here—worse than any Tx, 5x, or pairs 66-99—folding is the disciplined and correct decision to preserve chips.

I am currently in the lead and have won the first three hands by being aggressive. Holding 8-5 offsuit on the button, I have a weak hand, but positional advantage is key in heads-up play. Pro has shown a tendency to fold to pre-flop and flop aggression so far. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to continue applying pressure and see if I can take the pot down immediately or play a small pot in position.

I am in the Big Blind with K5o against an aggressive opponent who has raised the button frequently. Flash raised to 300. I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 600, offering me 2:1 odds. K5o has sufficient equity against a wide heads-up opening range to defend. 3-betting is unnecessary as I dominate some of their bluffing range and want to keep those hands in. Folding is too weak given the heads-up dynamic. I will call and see a flop.

I hold bottom pair with a strong kicker (K5 on A-6-5 board) out of position in a heads-up pot. The Ace favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, making a donk bet unwise as it likely only gets called by better hands while folding out bluffs. My hand has showdown value but is vulnerable. Checking is the standard line to evaluate the opponent's action, aiming to catch bluffs or pot control.

I have bottom pair (fives) on an Ace-high board. While I could bet to represent the Ace, checking back allows me to realize my equity and keep the pot small with a hand that has some showdown value but is vulnerable. Pro has been playing cautiously, and checking back protects my range and prevents me from being check-raised off my pair.

On the Turn, the board is [5d As 6c 8s]. The opponent checked back the flop, which often caps their range, suggesting they likely do not have a strong Ace. I hold a pair of Fives with a King kicker. While I have showdown value, my hand is not strong enough to value bet confidently, as I am unlikely to get called by worse hands (like 44, 33, or high cards) often enough to justify it, and betting might fold out the bluffs I beat while isolating me against better hands (Ax, 6x, 8x, 77+). Checking allows me to keep the pot small, realize my equity, and potentially induce bluffs from the opponent's air range. If the opponent bets, I will evaluate based on sizing, but for now, checking is the standard line to get to showdown cheaply.

On the turn, I have improved to two pair (eights and fives). Pro has checked twice, suggesting a weak range or a marginal made hand like a six or a low pocket pair. Since the board now contains several straight draws (7-9, 4-7) and I checked back the flop, my hand is well-disguised. I need to bet now to extract value from weaker pairs and to charge any draws. A bet of 400 into a 600 pot is a strong value sizing that Pro may still call with an Ace or a mid-pair.

I hold a pair of Fives with a King kicker on a board of [5d As 6c 8s]. The opponent checked back the flop, which often caps their range (making a strong Ace less likely). Now they are employing a delayed c-bet of 400 into 600. Given the heads-up dynamic and the opponent's aggressive pre-flop tendencies, they could be betting with a wide range including draws (spades, 7-9) or weaker pairs (6x, 8x) or trying to steal the pot with air. I need roughly 28% equity to call. My hand beats all pure bluffs and is live against some value hands if I improve on the river. Folding bottom pair against a button open who checked the flop feels too weak. Raising achieves little. I will call to see the river.

I hold bottom pair with a King kicker on a board of [5d As 6c 8s 9d]. The river card completes a straight for any 7x (specifically 79) and improves 9x, but the flush draw from the turn missed. Leading out (donk betting) has no value as worse hands (missed draws) will fold and better hands (Ax, 8x, 9x, 6x) will call or raise. My hand serves as a bluff-catcher. The optimal play is to check to the aggressor. This allows me to potentially win at showdown if they check back with air, or evaluate a bet if they decide to bluff their missed draws. Checking maximizes EV by keeping the pot size controlled and giving the opponent an opportunity to bluff.

I have two pair, Eights and Fives. The river 9d is a potential scare card as it completes a straight for any hand containing a Seven. However, Pro has checked to me for the third time this hand (excluding the turn call), which often indicates a marginal made hand like a weak Ace or a pair of Sixes rather than a monster. I will place a value bet of 700, which is half the pot, to extract maximum value from Ace-x hands. If Pro raises, I will have to carefully consider folding as the straight is a distinct possibility.

On the river, the board is [5d As 6c 8s 9d]. I hold a pair of Fives (bottom pair) with a King kicker. The opponent checked back the flop but has now fired two bullets (Turn and River). The river card (9d) is bad for my hand as it is an overcard to my pair and completes straights for any 7x (which was a valid semi-bluff on the turn). The opponent's line of checking the flop and betting the turn and river often represents either a delayed strong hand, a turned/rivered pair, or a bluff. However, my hand is extremely weak (4th pair). I lose to all value hands, including thin value bets like 6x or 8x, and I only beat pure bluffs (missed spades or air). Given the pot odds of 3:1, I need to be good 25% of the time. Since I lose to the vast majority of the opponent's range (Ax, 9x, 8x, 6x, 7x), and because I am at the very bottom of my bluff-catching range (I would prefer to call with 8x or 6x), folding is the disciplined and correct play to preserve my stack.

I hold 98o on the Button (SB) in a Heads-Up match. This hand is connected and has reasonable playability in position, falling within the top ~50-55% of hands, making it too strong to fold pre-flop. Given that the opponent (Flash) has shown aggression by raising my limp in Hand 1 and 3-betting in Hand 3, limping is likely to invite a raise, putting me in a difficult spot. By raising to 200 myself, I take the initiative, putting pressure on the Big Blind and allowing me to play the pot in position with the betting lead. If facing a 3-bet, I can easily fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 5s 7h. Pro has opened for a 2x raise to 200. I need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 pot odds. While 75o is a weak hand, it is generally worth a defend in heads-up play against a min-raise, even though I will be out of position post-flop. I will call to see the flop and evaluate based on board texture.

On a paired board of 4-3-3, I have a gutshot straight draw (needs a 6). Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to the raiser. This board is quite dry and may have missed Pro's opening range, but I have no reason to lead out with 7-high. I will check and see how Pro proceeds.

I am in position on a low, paired board of [4c 3s 3d] with 9-high. While I have the pre-flop initiative, this texture interacts reasonably well with the Big Blind's calling range (which includes many 3x, 4x, and low pocket pairs). My hand has very little equity if called, and better hands like Ace-high or King-high might not fold to a small continuation bet on this dry board. Additionally, the opponent has shown aggression in previous hands, increasing the risk of a check-raise. Checking back is the prudent play to control the pot size, realize any equity I have with my overcards for free, and re-evaluate on the turn.

The turn brings a third 3 to the board, making the board 4c-3s-3d-3h. I have 7-high, which currently plays as three 3s with a 7 kicker. Pro checked back the flop, suggesting they likely don't have a 4 or a 3, but they could still have many hands that beat mine (any pocket pair or any hand with a kicker higher than 7). Given that I have almost no showdown value and the board is very static, I will check to see how Pro reacts. There is no reason to turn my hand into a bluff yet, as most of Pro's range contains cards higher than my 7.

I hold 9-high on a board of [4c 3s 3d 3h]. The board now contains trip Threes. My hand rank is effectively Trip Threes with a 9-8 kicker. My opponent defended their Big Blind and has checked to me twice. Given the board texture, my opponent's range contains many overcards (A-high to T-high) and pocket pairs that are currently beating my 9-high. Betting here turns my hand into a bluff, but it is unlikely to fold out better hands like King-high or Ace-high which will likely call one street on this texture, and stronger hands like a 4 or a pocket pair will certainly continue. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, control the pot size, and potentially get to showdown cheaply or improve on the river.

The board has completed as a full house (3-3-3-4-4). Since my hole cards (7 and 5) are lower than the cards on the board, I am playing the board's full house. Pro has checked twice, which suggests they likely do not have a 4 or the case 3. I will check to reach a showdown; if Pro does not have a 4 or 3, we will split the pot. Betting here serves no purpose as I would only be called by a hand that beats me or by a hand that also plays the board for a chop.

The board reads [4c 3s 3d 3h 4s], which forms a Full House (Threes full of Fours) using the five community cards. My hole cards (9d 8s) do not improve this, so I am 'playing the board'. Any opponent holding a pocket pair 55 or higher, a 4, or a 3 beats me. I chop with any other hand (unpaired hands with no 4 or 3, or pocket 2s). Since I cannot get called by worse hands, and it is difficult to fold out a chop (opponents often call with Ace-high to split), betting has high risk with little reward. Checking back allows me to realize my share of the pot if we are chopping and avoids losing chips to a trap (like a slow-played 4 or pocket pair).

I am on the Button/Small Blind with Ten-Seven offsuit. Based on the previous hands, the opponent (Pro) has been relatively cautious, folding to several of my pre-flop raises. T7o is a standard opening hand in heads-up play from this position. I will raise to 300 to maintain my aggressive image and try to take down the blinds or control the hand with position.

In heads-up play, pocket pairs have significant value, even low ones like 22. My opponent, Flash, has been consistently opening to 300 (3bb) from the button. I am getting odds of 2:1 (calling 200 to win 600 total), requiring roughly 33% equity. Pocket deuces have approximately 50% equity against a wide opening range. While playing 22 out of position is difficult without hitting a set, folding it pre-flop would be too tight given the wide ranges in heads-up. 3-betting is risky as it bloats the pot with a hand that plays poorly post-flop if called. The most prudent play is to call and look to hit a set or a favorable low board.

I hold pocket deuces on a Q-9-6 rainbow board. This flop connects reasonably well with the button's raising range (Queens, Nines, straight draws like JT/87). My hand is an underpair with only two outs to improve to a set, making it very vulnerable. Being out of position with a weak hand that has little showdown value, checking is the standard play. I intend to fold if facing a standard continuation bet, as I do not have the equity to float or call down.

On a flop of Qd-9c-6h, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8) and two overcards to the 6. As the pre-flop aggressor, I will make a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to represent the Queen and put pressure on the opponent's range, which likely missed this board. Given the opponent's tendency to fold in previous hands, this bet has high fold equity.

I hold pocket deuces (2h 2s) on a Q-9-6 rainbow flop. My opponent, Flash, has continued aggression by betting 300 into a pot of 600 (half-pot). While 22 was a defendable hand pre-flop due to pot odds, it is now an underpair on a board that connects reasonably well with a Button opening range (Queens, Nines, straight draws like JT/87). I am currently losing to all value hands (Qx, 9x, 6x, and higher pocket pairs) and am flipping at best against draws. Even against pure overcard bluffs, it will be extremely difficult to realize equity out of position on future streets, as almost any turn card will be an overcard to my pair. With only 2 outs to improve to a set and facing a standard continuation bet, the expected value of continuing is negative. Folding is the disciplined decision.

In Heads-Up play, 98o is a decent hand (approximately top 48% of hands) and well within the standard opening range from the Button. Although the opponent has shown aggression with 3-bets (Hand #3) and raises over limps (Hand #1), playing too passively by folding or limping weak-medium hands allows the opponent to dominate. In Hand #5, I raised with 98s and the opponent called, leading to a manageable post-flop scenario. I will min-raise to 200 to assert initiative and utilize my position post-flop. If the opponent 3-bets, I can comfortably fold this specific hand.

I have a premium starting hand, Ace-King offsuit, in a heads-up situation. The opponent has raised from the button to 200. Given the strength of my hand and the need to build a pot while I have a significant range advantage, I will put in a 3-bet. Based on previous hands, the opponent has shown a willingness to fold to aggression. A raise to 700 is a standard sizing that applies pressure and extracts value from dominated hands.

I am facing a large 3-bet to 700 (7bb) from the Big Blind after opening to 200. The opponent 'Flash' has shown significant aggression throughout the match (3-betting Hand #3, barreling Hand #4). The pot odds require me to call 500 to win a pot of 900 (approx 36% equity required), but 98o is a marginal hand that plays poorly in inflated pots, even with position. It is easily dominated by the opponent's 3-betting range (Ax, Kx, pairs). As stated in my pre-flop plan, 98o is a comfortable fold against a 3-bet. Preserving chips for better spots is the prudent play here.

I am on the button with a pocket pair (4s4h). In heads-up play, any pocket pair is a strong starting hand. My opponent has been relatively tight-aggressive, folding to my 3-bets but also showing aggression when they enter the pot. I want to build a pot and maintain initiative while being mindful of the effective stack depth. A standard opening raise of 3x the big blind is effective here to put pressure on the big blind and extract value from worse hands or induce folds.

I hold Pocket Tens (Tc Th) in the Big Blind, a premium hand in heads-up play. The opponent has been opening consistently to 300 (3bb) on the button, indicating a wide range. While flat-calling to trap or control the pot size is an option, playing out of position is difficult if overcards appear on the flop. A 3-bet is the stronger play here to extract value from lower pairs and broadway hands that the opponent might call with, as well as to deny equity to random holdings. Raising to 1000 (3.3x the open) is a standard size for a 3-bet out of position, asserting dominance and aiming to take down the pot pre-flop or build a value pot with a strong hand.

I have a pocket pair of fours on the button. My opponent has 3-bet to 1,000. In a heads-up match, pocket pairs are strong, but 44 is vulnerable to overcards. Folding here would be too tight, as Pro has shown aggression in previous hands and 44 has enough equity against a wide heads-up 3-betting range. 4-betting might force me into a difficult spot if Pro shoves, especially since the effective stack is around 70 big blinds. Calling allows me to see a flop in position and potentially set-mine or navigate the hand based on the board texture and Pro's post-flop aggression while keeping the pot manageable.

In this 3-bet pot, I hold Pocket Tens on a J-6-3 board with a flush draw. I am out of position. While I have an overpair to the 6 and 3, the Jack hits the opponent's 3-bet calling range (which often consists of broadway hands like KJs, QJs, AJs). If I bet, I likely fold out hands I dominate (like non-diamond AQ/AK) and get action primarily from hands that beat me (Jx) or strong draws. Checking allows me to exercise pot control, keep the opponent's range wide, and potentially bluff-catch against aggressive stabs or floats. Given the opponent's aggressive history, checking to induce or control the size of the pot is the prudent play with a marginal made hand OOP.

I am holding pocket fours on a Jack-high board with a diamond draw. Pro 3-bet pre-flop but has now checked on the flop. This check could signal a variety of things: a slow-play with a strong Jack or an overpair, a pot-control move with a medium pair, or a complete miss with overcards like AK/AQ. Since I am in position and 44 is a marginal hand that is easily dominated or outdrawn, checking back is the most prudent play. It allows me to control the size of the pot, realize my equity, and see a turn card for free without risking a check-raise from a stronger range.

The turn brings the Ace of Spades [As], making the board [6d Jc 3d As]. This card heavily favors my perceived range as the pre-flop 3-bettor (containing AK, AQ, AA), but effectively downgrades my actual hand (Pocket Tens) to third pair. Since the opponent checked back the flop, their range is capped and likely consists of marginal made hands (like mid-pairs 77-99, weak Jx) or draws. Betting here would turn my hand into a bluff; I would fold out hands I currently beat (like 77-99) and isolate myself against better hands (Ax, Jx) that will call. By checking, I exercise pot control, intending to get to showdown cheaply. I keep the opponent's range wide, allowing them to potentially bluff with missed draws or check back with worse pairs that I can beat at showdown.

On the turn, the Ace of Spades is a significant card. My opponent, who 3-bet pre-flop, has now checked both the flop and the turn. While the Ace could have helped their range (AK, AQ), their double check suggests they either missed completely, are pot-controlling a medium pair (like 77-TT), or are trapping. My pocket fours have very little showdown value but are currently ahead of total air. However, betting here is unlikely to fold out a better hand given the pre-flop action, and checking allows me to see a free river and potentially win at showdown if the board doesn't improve their specific holdings. I will check back to control the pot size.

I arrive at the river with Pocket Tens on a board of [6d Jc 3d As 9s]. The action has gone check-check on both the flop and the turn. The opponent's decision to check back the turn, despite the Ace appearing, strongly caps their range; they likely do not have a strong Ace or a strong Jack, as those hands would typically bet for value or protection. Their range likely consists of marginal made hands (like 77-99, weak Jx, or weak Ax that is pot-controlling) or missed draws. Betting here has negative value: I would likely fold out the hands I beat (like 77-88, KQ) and isolate myself against the hands that beat me (weak Ax, Jx, 99). Checking is the best option to realize my showdown equity. If the opponent checks back, I often win against lower pairs or missed high cards. If they bet, I can re-evaluate, but my primary goal is a cheap showdown.

I am holding pocket fours in a 3-bet pot. The board texture (J-6-3-A-9) is not particularly favorable for my hand, but my opponent has checked on every street after showing pre-flop aggression. This indicates they likely missed the board or are holding a marginal hand they want to take to showdown cheaply. My pocket fours have very little showdown value, but they can occasionally win against missed high-card hands like KQ or diamond draws. Betting here as a bluff is unlikely to be successful against any pair, and I won't get called by any worse hands. Therefore, checking back is the most sensible play to see if my hand is good without risking more chips.

I hold 2h 6s on the Button (SB), which is one of the weakest starting hands in Hold'em. My opponent, Flash, has demonstrated aggressive tendencies in previous hands, frequently raising over limps and 3-betting my opens. Playing this hand, even in position, carries a high risk of losing more chips against an aggressive opponent. The most prudent play is to minimize losses by folding the small blind.

I am holding AQo on the button, which is a premium starting hand in heads-up play. My opponent has shown a tendency to fold to pre-flop aggression in previous hands. I will put in a standard 3x raise to 300 to build the pot and take advantage of my position and hand strength.

The opponent is on the Button/SB and has raised to 300 (3BB), which has been their consistent opening size. While Q2s is a suited Queen, it is the bottom of my range. In a Heads-Up scenario, I need approximately 25% equity to call (calling 200 to win 600), which Q2s raw equity satisfies against a wide range. However, playing Q2s Out of Position (OOP) against a 3x raise is difficult due to poor playability and reverse implied odds with the weak kicker. Folding prevents bleeding chips in a spot where equity realization is challenging.

I hold J4o on the Button (SB), which is a very weak hand (bottom 30% of range). My opponent 'Flash' has shown aggression pre-flop, frequently raising. Limping invites an isolation raise which would force a fold, wasting chips. Raising as a bluff is risky against an active opponent with a trash hand. The most prudent play at this stack depth and early stage is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I have 6d 2d on the button. While it's a weak hand, Pro has shown a high frequency of folding to pre-flop raises in earlier hands. Maintaining my aggressive image and attempting to take the blinds is a standard play here.

I hold ATo in the Big Blind against a Button raise to 300 (3BB). The opponent (Flash) has been opening frequently (3x sizing), suggesting a wide range. ATo is significantly ahead of a standard Button opening range in heads-up play. I have excellent pot odds to call (calling 200 to win a pot that will be 600). While a 3-bet is a viable option to take the initiative, calling is the standard play to keep the opponent's range wide and avoid getting blown off a solid hand by a 4-bet while out of position. I will call and evaluate the flop.

I hold ATo on a wet Qh-9d-8h board. I have an open-ended straight draw (needs a Jack or 7) and an overcard (Ace). Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to the raiser. This allows me to see if my opponent continues aggression. If they bet, I have a strong enough hand to continue (call or check-raise); if they check, I get a free card to try and hit my straight or Ace. Donk betting here is unnecessary and exposes my hand strength too early.

I am on the button with 6d 2d. The flop is Qh 9d 8h, which is quite connected and likely hits a lot of a Big Blind defending range. However, looking at the previous hands (especially Hand 3 and Hand 6), the opponent 'Pro' has shown a tendency to fold to continuation bets on the flop after calling pre-flop. By betting half the pot here, I am maintaining my aggressive image and putting pressure on any hands that missed this flop, such as small pairs or high-card air like AK or AJ. Since I have 6-high, I have almost no showdown value, so winning the pot now is preferable to checking and hoping to hit a runner-runner.

I hold ATo on a wet Qh-9d-8h board. Despite the previous thought mentioning an OESD, strictly speaking with a Ten on this board, I have a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (needing a Jack) and an Ace overcard. I also hold the Ace of hearts, giving me a backdoor nut flush draw. Facing a half-pot c-bet (300 into 600) from an aggressive opponent in a heads-up match, I am getting 3:1 pot odds (calling 300 to win 900+300). My hand has sufficient equity (approx. 7 clean outs plus backdoors) and implied odds to justify a call. Folding is too weak given the wide ranges, and raising would isolate me against better hands while folding out bluffs I beat.

I hold ATo on a [Qh 9d 8h 3d] board. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack) and an Ace overcard. The 3d on the turn is likely a brick that doesn't change the board texture significantly, though it adds a second flush draw (diamonds) that I don't have. As the pre-flop caller playing out of position against an aggressive opponent who c-bet the flop, the standard line is to check. Donk betting here is unlikely to get value from worse hands and exposes me to raises. I will check to the raiser and evaluate their action.

The turn 3d is a favorable card for my range and significantly increases my equity by providing a flush draw to go with my air. Pro's check after calling my flop continuation bet on a highly connected board (Q-9-8) often indicates a capped range, likely consisting of marginal one-pair hands or straight draws looking for a cheap river. By firing a second barrel of 700 into the 1200 pot, I apply maximum pressure on these hands and maintain the aggressive image I've established. Even if called, I have nine outs to a flush which allows me to potentially take down a large pot on the river.

On the turn, the board is [Qh 9d 8h 3d]. I hold [Ts Ah], giving me a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack) and an Ace overcard. My opponent bets 700 into a pot of 1900. To call, I need to contribute 700 to a total pot of 2600, requiring approximately 27% equity. My gutshot provides 4 outs (Jacks) and the Ace overcard adds at most 3 outs (discounted as Top Pair might not be good against two-pair or sets), giving me roughly 14% equity (7 outs * 2). Since my equity is significantly lower than the direct pot odds and I am out of position against an aggressive opponent who is likely to fire the river, continuing is negative EV.

In Heads-Up play, Q4s is a respectable starter hand on the Button, well within the top 50-60% of hands usually opened. My opponent 'Flash' has shown aggression with 3-bets in previous hands (Hand #3, #7), but folding suited broadway/connector type hands like Q4s is too passive. Limping is risky given Flash raised my limp in Hand #1. A minimum raise to 200 allows me to play the pot in position if called, and it's a small enough investment to fold if faced with a 3-bet. I will raise to steal the initiative.

I am holding Ten-Eight of spades, a strong suited hand for heads-up play. Pro has raised to 200 from the Button. Given my stack lead and Pro's tendency to fold to previous 3-bets, I will put in a 3-bet to 600 to apply pressure and take control of the hand out of position. This allows me to win the pot immediately or set up a strong continuation play on the flop.

I hold Ace-Jack suited (Ah Jh) on the button, a premium hand in heads-up play. I initially raised to 200, and my opponent 3-bet to 700. After my flop continuation bet was called, the turn 5s is a blank that does not improve my Ace-Jack. I hold Ah-Jh on a board of 3d-Th-Qd-5s. After calling the flop bet, the pot is 2600 and my opponent checks the turn. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a King) and Ace-high. On the river board of 3d-Th-Qd-5s-2c, I am holding Ace-high (Ac Jc). After 3-betting pre-flop and leading the flop, the turn went check-check. By checking, I can potentially win the pot at showdown if Pro checks back with a hand like KJ or J9, or I can safely fold if Pro decides to bet, as my Ace-high is unlikely to be the best hand if significant action occurs on this river.

I hold Ah-Jh on a board of 3d-Th-Qd-5s-2c. The pot is 2600. This was a 3-bet pot pre-flop. I called a flop c-bet, and the turn went check-check. On the river, Flash checks again. K8o is a strong hand for heads-up play, especially from the button. I'll maintain my aggressive pre-flop strategy by raising to 300, looking to take control of the pot early or force a fold from the big blind.

I am in the Big Blind with [7d Ks] facing a Turn bet of 600 into a pot of 1200 on a board of [2s 3s Qd 6h]. Folding forfeits too much equity, and raising (check-raise) bloats the pot with only a draw. Calling is the most prudent play to realize my equity and potentially win a large pot if I hit my flush.
